reasoning: Candidate A has the stronger desktop first viewport: the alert form, restaurant/date/party inputs, no-login trust trio, sample alert, and proof bar are all visible and conversion-ready. But A fails more deterministic gates: missing/unjustified components, Section 12 count mismatch, and mobile horizontal overflow. Its mobile screenshot also crops the nav/login area and pushes the primary CTA below the first viewport, weakening the mobile conversion path. Candidate B has a less efficient desktop first fold because the form/CTA stack is lower and the extra full-width CTA creates some spatial awkwardness, but it preserves the core mobile path: headline, no-login trust, primary CTA, risk reducer, and sample alert preview are reachable without layout breakage. B also executes the segment-self-selection strategy more faithfully across the full page with separate family, superfan, pricing, and agency paths, while A's workflow-first strategy mostly reads as a strong action-first landing page with a conventional how-it-works section below. Both have trope exposure from equal card rows, pricing cards, testimonial cards, and FAQ-like sections, but A is more dominated by generic SaaS section sequencing and has worse programmatic/render risk.
best_reusable_move_a: The desktop hero is the best reusable move: split copy plus embedded alert widget plus trust trio and compact alert preview gives high-intent users a real setup path while resolving no-login and official-handoff concerns immediately.
best_reusable_move_b: The segment-specific depth sequence is the best reusable move: family safety proof, superfan cadence/comparison tables, and agency inquiry are separated cleanly so expert and professional users get relevant proof without polluting the hero.
